Superstore Marketing Campaign Analysis

Customer Segmentation & Membership Response Prediction

Business Problem Statement

A retail superstore company wants to analyze customer purchasing behaviour, campaign engagement, and product spending patterns to improve targeted marketing strategies and increase membership acceptance rates.

The company collects customer demographic details, purchase history, website activity, and spending information across multiple product categories. However, management lacks clear insights into which customer segments contribute the most revenue and which factors influence campaign response.

This project focuses on identifying high-value customers, understanding purchasing behaviour, predicting membership acceptance, and generating data-driven recommendations using Python, SQL, and Power BI.

Project Objectives

The primary objective of this project is to analyze customer behaviour and identify the key factors influencing membership campaign acceptance. The analysis aims to support data-driven marketing decisions and improve customer engagement strategies.

  • Analyze customer demographics and purchasing behaviour
  • Identify high-value and low-engagement customer segments
  • Study spending patterns across multiple product categories
  • Examine customer activity through web, catalog, and store purchases
  • Perform exploratory data analysis (EDA) to discover hidden insights
  • Build predictive models to forecast campaign response
  • Create interactive dashboards using Power BI
  • Generate actionable business recommendations for marketing optimization

Dataset Description

The dataset used in this project contains customer demographic information, purchasing behaviour, product spending patterns, and campaign response details collected from a retail superstore marketing campaign. The dataset consists of 2,240 customer records and 22 attributes related to customer profile, income, purchasing channels, spending categories, website activity, and campaign acceptance behaviour. The primary goal of the dataset is to analyze customer engagement and predict whether a customer will accept the Gold Membership marketing campaign.

Key Dataset Features

  • Customer Demographics: Age, Education, Marital Status, Income
  • Family Information: Kidhome, Teenhome
  • Spending Behaviour: Wine, Meat, Fish, Fruits, Sweet, and Gold product spending
  • Purchase Channels: Web purchases, Catalog purchases, Store purchases
  • Customer Engagement: Website visits and Recency of purchases
  • Target Variable: Response (Campaign Accepted or Not)

Importing Required Libraries

The project uses Python libraries for data manipulation, visualization, statistical analysis, and machine learning.

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

Data Loading

The dataset was imported into the Python environment using the Pandas library for further preprocessing and analysis.

Data Cleaning

Missing values were handled, duplicate records were verified, and inconsistent entries were corrected during preprocessing.

In [5]:
# Filling Missing Values With Median
#----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
supermarket_data["Income"].fillna(supermarket_data["Income"].median(), inplace=True)

#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# checking missing values
#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("MissingValues:\n",supermarket_data.isnull().sum())

#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Checking Duplicated values
#-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("\nDuplicateValues:",supermarket_data.duplicated().sum())

MntFruits              0

NumWebPurchases        0
NumCatalogPurchases    0
NumStorePurchases      0
NumWebVisitsMonth      0
Response               0
Complain               0
dtype: int64

DuplicateValues: 0

Date Transformation

The customer enrollment date column was converted into datetime format for temporal analysis and feature engineering.

In [6]:
# Converting date colummn data type text to date
#-----------------------------------------------------------
supermarket_data["Dt_Customer"] = pd.to_datetime(
    supermarket_data["Dt_Customer"],
     format = "%m/%d/%y"
)
#-------------------------------------------------------------
# chechking data type
#--------------------------------------------------------------
print(supermarket_data["Dt_Customer"].dtype)

datetime64[ns]

Feature Engineering

New analytical features such as Age, Total Spend, and Customer Tenure were created to improve customer behavior analysis and predictive modeling.

In [7]:
# Creatig new columns
#--------------------------------------------------------
# Age column
#--------------------------------------------------------
supermarket_data["Age"] = 2014 - supermarket_data["Year_Birth"]

#--------------------------------------------------------
# Total Spend column
#--------------------------------------------------------
supermarket_data["Total_Spend"] = (
    supermarket_data["MntWines"] +
    supermarket_data["MntFruits"] +
    supermarket_data["MntMeatProducts"] +
    supermarket_data["MntFishProducts"] +
    supermarket_data["MntSweetProducts"] +
    supermarket_data["MntGoldProds"]
)

#-------------------------------------------------------
# Customer Tenure column 
#-------------------------------------------------------
supermarket_data["Customer_Tenure"] = 2014 - supermarket_data["Dt_Customer"].dt.year
In [8]:
supermarket_data.shape
Out[8]:
(2240, 25)

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) was performed to understand customer demographics, purchasing behaviour, spending patterns, campaign engagement, and customer interaction trends.

The analysis helps identify meaningful business insights, customer segments, and factors influencing campaign response behaviour.

Various statistical summaries and visualizations were used to analyze relationships between income, age, purchasing channels, product spending categories, and customer engagement metrics.

Demographic Analysis

Demographic analysis is performed to understand the customer profile based on age, education level, marital status, and income distribution. Analyzing customer demographics helps identify the characteristics of different customer groups and supports targeted marketing and customer engagement strategies.

This section focuses on understanding customer composition across different demographic factors such as age groups, educational background, marital status, and income levels.

Age Distribution Analysis

Customer age distribution was analyzed to identify dominant age groups and understand how age influences customer purchasing behaviour and campaign response patterns.

In [9]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14,5))

# Age Distribution
sns.histplot(
    supermarket_data["Age"],
    kde=True,
    color="#0F4C81",
    line_kws = {"color":"#123B6D","linewidth":3},
    ax=axes[0]
)

axes[0].set_title("Age Distribution", fontsize=14)
axes[0].set_xlabel("Age")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Customer Count")

# Age Boxplot
sns.boxplot(
    x=supermarket_data["Age"],
    color="#123B6D",
    ax=axes[1]
)

axes[1].set_title("Age Boxplot", fontsize=14)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Age")


plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The age distribution indicates that the majority of customers fall within the middle-aged category, particularly between the age range of 35 to 60 years. The histogram shows that customer representation is strongest among mature adults, while younger and older age groups contribute comparatively less to the overall customer base.

The distribution appears moderately right-skewed, suggesting the presence of a smaller population of older customers. The boxplot further confirms this pattern and highlights a few extreme age outliers with unusually high values exceeding 100 years. These observations indicate potential inconsistencies or data quality issues in the Year_Birth column, which may require additional validation during the data cleaning phase.

The median age lies around the late 40s, showing that experienced and financially stable customers form the primary target audience for the retailer. The interquartile range suggests moderate variability in customer ages, indicating a relatively balanced spread across adult customer segments.

Overall, the analysis suggests that the retailer’s customer base is dominated by middle-aged consumers, making this segment highly valuable for targeted promotions, membership campaigns, and personalized marketing strategies.

Education and Marital Status Distribution Analysis

Demographic distribution analysis is performed to understand the educational background and marital status composition of customers. Analyzing these demographic factors helps identify the characteristics of the customer base and provides insights into customer diversity and social profiles.

This analysis helps businesses better understand their target audience and supports the development of more personalized marketing strategies, customer engagement approaches, and customer segmentation initiatives.

In [10]:
# Education and Marital Status Distribution Analysis

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(16, 6))


# Education Distribution

education_counts = supermarket_data["Education"].value_counts()

bars1 = axes[0].bar(
    education_counts.index,
    education_counts.values,
    color="#123458",
    edgecolor="black"
)

axes[0].set_title("Education Distribution", fontsize=14)
axes[0].set_xlabel("Education")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Count")
axes[0].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=20)

for bar in bars1:
    
    height = bar.get_height()
    
    axes[0].text(
        bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2,
        height,
        int(height),
        ha='center',
        va='bottom',
        fontsize=10
    )


# Marital Status Distribution

marital_counts = supermarket_data["Marital_Status"].value_counts()

bars2 = axes[1].bar(
    marital_counts.index,
    marital_counts.values,
    color="#4F81BD",
    edgecolor="black"
)

axes[1].set_title("Marital Status Distribution", fontsize=14)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Marital Status")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Count")
axes[1].tick_params(axis='x', rotation=20)

for bar in bars2:
    
    height = bar.get_height()
    
    axes[1].text(
        bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2,
        height,
        int(height),
        ha='center',
        va='bottom',
        fontsize=10
    )

sns.despine()

plt.tight_layout()

plt.show()

The demographic distribution analysis highlights the educational background and marital status composition of customers. Understanding these demographic characteristics helps identify the dominant customer groups and supports the development of targeted marketing strategies and personalized customer engagement approaches.

The education distribution indicates that the majority of customers belong to the Graduation category, followed by PhD and Master degree holders. This suggests that the customer base is largely composed of well-educated individuals, which may influence purchasing behaviour, campaign responsiveness, and product preferences.

The marital status distribution shows that married customers form the largest customer segment, followed by customers living together and single customers. This indicates that family-oriented and household customers contribute significantly to the business customer base.

Overall, the demographic analysis provides valuable insights into customer composition and helps businesses better understand the characteristics of their primary customer segments for more effective marketing and customer relationship strategies.

Income Distribution Analysis

Income distribution analysis was performed to understand customer earning patterns and identify variations in purchasing power among different customer groups.

The analysis helps evaluate whether higher-income customers contribute more toward product spending and campaign engagement.

In [11]:
# Income Distribution & Income Boxplot
# Create figure with 2 rows and 1 column
#--------------------------------------------------
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(10,8))
#----------------------------------------------------
# Income Distribution Plot
# Histogram to understand income distribution
#-----------------------------------------------------
sns.histplot(
    supermarket_data["Income"],
    kde=True,
    color="#0F4C81",
    line_kws = {"color":"#123B6D","linewidth":3},

    ax=axes[0]
)
#---------------------------------------------------
# Title and axis labels
axes[0].set_title("Income Distribution", fontsize=14)
axes[0].set_xlabel("Income")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Customer Count")
#-----------------------------------------------------
# Income Boxplot
# Boxplot to detect spread and outliers
#-----------------------------------------------------
sns.boxplot(
    x=supermarket_data["Income"],
    color="#123B6D",
    ax=axes[1]
)

# Title and axis label
axes[1].set_title("Income Boxplot", fontsize=14)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Income")

# Adjust layout and display charts
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The income distribution shows a strong right-skewed pattern, indicating that the majority of customers belong to the low-to-middle income segment, while a comparatively smaller group earns significantly higher income. Most customers appear concentrated within a moderate income range, suggesting that the retailer primarily serves middle-income households.

The boxplot further highlights the presence of several high-income outliers. A few customers have exceptionally large income values, extending far beyond the typical income range of the overall customer base. These premium customers may contribute disproportionately to overall sales and could represent an important segment for targeted marketing and membership campaigns.

The wide spread of income values reflects the diversity of the retailer’s customer base across different economic groups. While most customers exhibit moderate purchasing power, the existence of affluent customers creates opportunities for personalized promotions, premium product recommendations, and loyalty-based engagement strategies.

Overall, the visual analysis indicates a customer population dominated by middle-income consumers, with a smaller but strategically valuable high-income segment that may significantly influence campaign response and spending behaviour.

Spending and Purchase Behaviour Analysis

Spending and purchase behaviour analysis is performed to understand customer purchasing patterns, product preferences, and shopping channel behaviour. This analysis helps identify how customers contribute to overall business revenue and which product categories and purchase channels generate the highest customer activity.

The analysis focuses on total customer spending, product category-wise spending patterns, and customer purchase behaviour across different purchasing channels such as web, store, and catalog purchases.

Total Spending Analysis

Customer spending behaviour was analyzed using the Total Spend feature created during feature engineering.The analysis identifies high-value customers and evaluates overall purchasing patterns across multiple product categories.

In [12]:
# Total Spending Distribution & Boxplot

# Create figure with 1 row and 2 columns
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14,5))


# Total Spending Distribution

# Histogram to understand spending behaviour
sns.histplot(
    supermarket_data["Total_Spend"],
    kde=True,
    color="#0F4C81",
    line_kws={"color":"#00008B", "linewidth":3},
    ax=axes[0]
)

# Title and labels
axes[0].set_title("Total Spending Distribution", fontsize=14)
axes[0].set_xlabel("Total Spend")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Customer Count")

# Total Spending Boxplot

# Boxplot to detect outliers
sns.boxplot(
    x=supermarket_data["Total_Spend"],
    color="#123B6D",
    ax=axes[1]
)

# Title and labels
axes[1].set_title("Total Spending Boxplot", fontsize=14)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Total Spend")


# Adjust layout
plt.tight_layout()

# Show charts
plt.show()

The total spending analysis reveals a highly right-skewed distribution, indicating that the majority of customers spend relatively lower amounts, while a smaller segment contributes significantly higher spending values. Most customers are concentrated within the lower spending range, suggesting that regular or moderate buyers form the core customer base of the retailer.

The histogram shows a gradual decline in customer frequency as total spending increases, highlighting the presence of fewer high-spending customers. This pattern is commonly observed in retail businesses, where a limited number of premium customers contribute disproportionately to overall revenue generation.

The boxplot further confirms the existence of high-value outliers with exceptionally large spending amounts. These customers represent a strategically important segment for the business, as they are likely to generate higher profitability and stronger long-term customer value. Identifying and retaining these high-spending customers can significantly improve campaign effectiveness and loyalty program performance.

The wide spread in spending behaviour also reflects the diversity of purchasing habits among customers. While many customers make occasional or moderate purchases, a smaller premium segment demonstrates consistently higher spending activity across product categories.

Overall, the analysis suggests that the retailer’s revenue is influenced by both a large base of moderate spenders and a smaller group of high-value customers. This creates opportunities for customer segmentation, personalized promotions, premium membership targeting, and loyalty-driven marketing strategies.

Product Category Spending Analysis

Spending patterns across different product categories such as Wines, Meat Products, Fruits, Fish Products, Sweets, and Gold Products were analyzed to identify the most preferred customer product segments.

In [13]:
# Product Category Spending Analysis

# Calculate total spending for each category
category_spending = supermarket_data[[
    "MntWines",
    "MntMeatProducts",
    "MntGoldProds",
    "MntFishProducts",
    "MntSweetProducts",
    "MntFruits"
]].sum().sort_values(ascending=False)


# Custom professional colors
custom_colors = [
    "#0F4C81",
    "#1769AA",
    "#1E8449",
    "#17A589",
    "#5DADE2",
    "#82E0AA"
]


# Create figure
plt.figure(figsize=(12,4))

# Barplot
sns.barplot(
    x=category_spending.index,
    y=category_spending.values,
    hue=category_spending.index,
    palette=custom_colors,
    legend=False
)

# Add values on bars
for index, value in enumerate(category_spending.values):
    plt.text(
        index,
        value + 10000,
        f"{value:,.0f}",
        ha='center',
        fontsize=10
    )

# Title and labels
plt.title("Product Category Spending Analysis", fontsize=15)
plt.xlabel("Product Categories")
plt.ylabel("Total Spending")

# Rotate x labels
plt.xticks(rotation=10)

# Remove top and right borders
sns.despine()

# Show chart
plt.show()

The product category spending analysis reveals significant differences in customer purchasing behaviour across product categories. Among all categories, Wine Products generated the highest total spending by a substantial margin, making it the dominant revenue-contributing category for the retailer. This indicates strong customer preference and high purchasing frequency within the wine segment.

Meat Products emerged as the second-highest spending category, although total expenditure remains considerably lower compared to wines. The strong performance of these two categories suggests that customers are willing to spend more on premium and lifestyle-oriented products, highlighting their importance in overall business profitability.

The remaining categories, including Gold Products, Fish Products, Sweet Products, and Fruits, contribute relatively smaller portions of total spending. While these categories generate lower revenue individually, they still play an important role in maintaining product diversity and supporting cross-category purchasing behaviour among customers.

The noticeable gap between top-performing and low-performing categories indicates unequal customer demand across product segments. This creates opportunities for the retailer to design category-specific promotional campaigns, personalized recommendations, and cross-selling strategies to improve engagement in underperforming categories.

Overall, the analysis suggests that customer spending is heavily concentrated in a few major product categories, particularly Wines and Meat Products. These categories represent critical drivers of sales performance and should remain a strategic focus for inventory planning, loyalty programs, and future marketing initiatives.

Purchase Channel Analysis

Customer purchasing behaviour was evaluated across multiple purchase channels including website purchases, catalog purchases, and in-store purchases.

The objective was to identify the most frequently used purchasing platform and understand customer shopping preferences.

In [14]:
# Purchase Channel Analysis

# Calculate total purchases across channels
channel_purchases = supermarket_data[[
    "NumWebPurchases",
    "NumCatalogPurchases",
    "NumStorePurchases"
]].sum().sort_values(ascending=False)


# Custom colors
channel_colors = [
    "#0F4C81",
    "#17A589",
    "#5DADE2"
]


# Create figure
plt.figure(figsize=(12,4))

# Barplot
sns.barplot(
    x=channel_purchases.index,
    y=channel_purchases.values,
    hue=channel_purchases.index,
    palette=channel_colors,
    legend=False
)

# Add values on bars
for index, value in enumerate(channel_purchases.values):
    plt.text(
        index,
        value + 1000,
        f"{value:,.0f}",
        ha='center',
        fontsize=10
    )

# Title and labels
plt.title("Purchase Channel Analysis", fontsize=15)
plt.xlabel("Purchase Channels")
plt.ylabel("Total Purchases")

# Rename labels
plt.xticks(
    ticks=[0,1,2],
    labels=["Store Purchases", "Web Purchases", "Catalog Purchases"]
)

# Remove top and right borders
sns.despine()

# Show chart
plt.show()

The purchase channel analysis highlights customer buying behaviour across different purchasing platforms, including physical stores, web platforms, and catalog purchases. The visualization clearly shows that Store Purchases contribute the highest number of transactions, making physical retail stores the dominant purchase channel for the company.

The strong performance of store purchases suggests that customers continue to prefer in-person shopping experiences, where they can directly explore products and make immediate purchasing decisions. This indicates that physical retail locations remain a major driver of customer engagement and sales performance.

Web Purchases represent the second-largest transaction channel, demonstrating significant customer adoption of online shopping platforms. The relatively high volume of web transactions indicates growing digital engagement and highlights the importance of maintaining a strong online presence and personalized digital marketing strategies.

Catalog Purchases contribute the lowest number of transactions among all purchase channels. Although this channel generates comparatively lower customer activity, it may still serve niche customer segments who prefer traditional purchasing methods or promotional catalogs.

The variation in purchase behaviour across channels reflects the diverse shopping preferences of customers. Some customers prefer convenience-driven online purchases, while others continue to rely on traditional in-store shopping experiences. Understanding these channel preferences can help the company optimize marketing campaigns, improve customer targeting, and allocate business resources more effectively.

Overall, the analysis suggests that physical stores remain the primary sales channel, while digital purchasing channels are steadily gaining importance. Strengthening omnichannel marketing strategies and enhancing the customer experience across all channels may further improve engagement, retention, and campaign conversion performance.

Customer Engagement Analysis

Customer engagement metrics such as website visits and recency of purchases were analyzed to evaluate customer activity levels and interaction patterns with the business.

In [15]:
# Customer Engagement Analysis

# Create Customer Engagement feature
supermarket_data["Total_Engagement"] = (
    supermarket_data["NumWebPurchases"] +
    supermarket_data["NumCatalogPurchases"] +
    supermarket_data["NumStorePurchases"] +
    supermarket_data["NumWebVisitsMonth"]
)


# Create figure with 1 row and 2 columns
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14,5))


# Engagement Distribution

# Histogram
sns.histplot(
    supermarket_data["Total_Engagement"],
    bins = 15,
    kde=True,
    color="#0F4C81",
    line_kws={"color":"#00008B", "linewidth":3},
    ax=axes[0]
)

# Title and labels
axes[0].set_title("Customer Engagement Distribution", fontsize=14)
axes[0].set_xlabel("Total Engagement")
axes[0].set_ylabel("Customer Count")


# Engagement Boxplot

# Boxplot
sns.boxplot(
    x=supermarket_data["Total_Engagement"],
    color="#17A589",
    ax=axes[1]
)

# Title and labels
axes[1].set_title("Customer Engagement Boxplot", fontsize=14)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Total Engagement")


# Adjust layout
plt.tight_layout()

# Show charts
plt.show()

The customer engagement analysis evaluates how actively customers interact with the retailer across different purchasing and digital channels. The Total Engagement feature combines store purchases, web purchases, catalog purchases, and monthly website visits to measure overall customer activity and engagement behaviour.

The histogram indicates that the majority of customers fall within the moderate engagement range, particularly between engagement scores of 10 to 25 . This suggests that most customers maintain regular interaction with the company through purchasing activity and website engagement, forming a stable and active customer base.

The distribution appears slightly right-skewed, indicating the presence of a smaller group of highly engaged customers with significantly higher interaction levels. These customers are likely to represent loyal or premium customer segments who frequently interact with the company across multiple channels.

The boxplot further confirms that customer engagement levels are relatively balanced, with no excessive extreme outliers observed in the dataset. The median engagement lies within the moderate range, suggesting that average customer interaction behaviour remains consistent across the majority of the customer base.

The presence of highly engaged customers creates valuable opportunities for loyalty programs, personalized recommendations, premium membership campaigns, and retention-focused marketing strategies. At the same time, customers with lower engagement levels may require targeted promotional efforts to increase activity and improve long-term customer relationships.

Overall, the analysis suggests that the retailer maintains a moderately engaged customer base with a smaller segment of highly active customers contributing significantly to overall interaction and business performance.

Campaign Response Analysis

Campaign response analysis is performed to evaluate customer reactions toward marketing campaigns and promotional offers. The analysis focuses on identifying differences between customers who accepted the campaign and those who did not respond positively to the offer.

This section helps understand customer response behaviour, campaign effectiveness, customer spending differences, and engagement patterns associated with marketing campaign acceptance.

Campaign Response Analysis

The campaign response analysis examines the number of customers who accepted and rejected the marketing campaign offer.This analysis helps measure customer engagement and evaluate campaign effectiveness.

In [16]:
# Campaign Response Count Plot
#---------------------------------------
plt.figure(figsize=(12,4))
sns.countplot(
    x="Response",
    data=supermarket_data,
    hue="Response",
    palette=["#C60C0C","#1E871B"],
    legend=False
)

# Add numbers on bars
for container in plt.gca().containers:
    plt.bar_label(container)

# Title and labels
plt.title("Campaign Response Count", fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel("Response")
plt.ylabel("")



# Rename x-axis values
plt.xticks([0,1], ["Not Accepted", "Accepted"])

sns.despine()

# Show chart
plt.show()

The campaign response analysis shows a significant imbalance between customers who accepted the Gold Membership offer and those who did not. A very large proportion of customers rejected the campaign, while only a comparatively smaller segment responded positively to the offer.

The visual clearly indicates that the current marketing campaign achieved limited conversion success. Although the campaign successfully reached a broad customer base, only a small percentage of customers demonstrated interest in accepting the membership program. This suggests that the campaign may not have been sufficiently personalized or targeted toward the most responsive customer groups.

The low acceptance count highlights the importance of customer segmentation and behavioural analysis. Factors such as income level, spending behaviour, purchase frequency, web engagement, and product preferences may strongly influence campaign acceptance rates. Identifying these patterns can help the company improve future marketing effectiveness.

The accepted customer segment, despite being smaller in size, may represent highly valuable customers with stronger purchasing intent and higher engagement levels. These customers can be further analyzed to design targeted retention strategies, premium offers, and loyalty-based campaigns.

Overall, the analysis suggests that the majority of customers are currently resistant to the membership offer, creating an opportunity for the retailer to refine campaign targeting, improve promotional strategies, and enhance customer engagement to increase future conversion rates.

Customer Response Behaviour Analysis

This section analyzes the behavioural differences between customers who accepted and those who did not accept the marketing campaign offer. The objective of this analysis is to identify the customer characteristics associated with positive campaign response.

Comparative visualizations are created using barplots and boxplots for important business variables such as Income, Total Spend, Total Engagement, and Number of Web Purchases. The barplots help compare average customer behaviour across both groups, while the boxplots provide insights into data distribution, spending variability, and customer activity patterns.

This analysis helps evaluate whether high-income, high-spending, highly engaged, and digitally active customers are more likely to respond positively to marketing campaigns.

In [17]:
# Response-Based Comparative Analysis
# Create figure with 4 rows and 2 columns
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 2, figsize=(16,20))
#-------------------------------------------------------
# Response vs Income
#-------------------------------------------------------
# Barplot
sns.barplot(
    x="Response",
    y="Income",
    data=supermarket_data,
    hue="Response",
    palette=["#0B1F3A", "#1F4E79"],
    errorbar=None,
    legend=False,
    ax=axes[0,0]
)
# Add values on bars
for container in axes[0,0].containers:
    axes[0,0].bar_label(container, fmt="%.0f")

# Title and Labels
axes[0,0].set_title("Average Income by Campaign Response", fontsize=13)
axes[0,0].set_xlabel("Campaign Response")
axes[0,0].set_ylabel("Average Income")

# Rename x-axis labels
axes[0,0].set_xticks([0,1])
axes[0,0].set_xticklabels(["Not Accepted", "Accepted"])
#-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Boxplot
sns.boxplot(
    x="Response",
    y="Income",
    data=supermarket_data,
    hue="Response",
    palette=["#B0C4DE", "#5F9EA0"],
    legend=False,
    ax=axes[0,1]
)

# Title and Labels
axes[0,1].set_title("Income Distribution by Campaign Response", fontsize=13)
axes[0,1].set_xlabel("Campaign Response")
axes[0,1].set_ylabel("Income")

# Rename x-axis labels
axes[0,1].set_xticks([0,1])
axes[0,1].set_xticklabels(["Not Accepted", "Accepted"])
#---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Response vs Total Spend
# ---------------------------------------------------------

sns.barplot(
    x="Response",
    y="Total_Spend",
    data=supermarket_data,

    legend=False,

)

    x="Response",

)

#----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

    axes[2,0].bar_label(container, fmt="%.1f")

# Rename x-axis labels

axes[2,0].set_xticklabels(["Not Accepted", "Accepted"])

    x="Response",
    y="Total_Engagement",
    data=supermarket_data,
    hue="Response",
